name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**ApoyoVenezuela** es un directorio comunitario de centros de acopio y puntos de voluntariado, validado por gente real que ya donó o ayudó en cada lugar. Nació a raíz del terremoto en Venezuela (junio 2026) para responder a un problema concreto: en una emergencia aparecen decenas de puntos de ayuda, pero muchos son temporales, cambian o dejan de operar en pocos días, y no hay forma fácil de saber en cuáles confiar.

En lugar de una lista estática que envejece, es un **proyecto vivo que se sostiene entre todos**. La comunidad lo mantiene al día con tres acciones:

- **Agregar** un lugar que conozcas, para que otros lo encuentren.
- **Confirmar** que estuviste ahí (donaste o fuiste voluntario). Cada confirmación le da confianza a quien viene después.
- **Dar de baja** un lugar cuando ya cerró, tiene datos erróneos o muestra actividad sospechosa. Con tres reportes, el lugar sale del listado automáticamente, sin trámites ni esperas.

## ¿Para quién es?

Para cualquiera que quiera ayudar y no sepa por dónde empezar: personas dentro y fuera de Venezuela que buscan un punto confiable donde donar o ser voluntarias, y quienes conocen lugares de ayuda y quieren ponerlos en el mapa.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Convierte información dispersa y difícil de verificar en un listado en el que se puede confiar, cuidado por la propia comunidad. Nadie depende de un administrador central: la gente que pisa los lugares es la que valida, corrige y depura el directorio en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Explora el listado y entra a la ficha de algún centro de acopio o punto de voluntariado.
2. Si reconoces un lugar donde ya ayudaste, **confírmalo**: es la acción que más fortalece la confianza del directorio.
3. ¿Sabes de un lugar que falta? **Agrégalo** en segundos.
4. Compártelo con quien esté buscando cómo ayudar 👉 [apoyovenezuela.org](https://www.apoyovenezuela.org)

analysis.summary: ApoyoVenezuela.org is a community-sourced, live directory of donation collection points and volunteering opportunities for Venezuelan earthquake victims abroad (diaspora-focused). Built with React 19 + Supabase, it is already deployed at apoyovenezuela.org and contains 17+ seeded real locations across Spain, Chile, Argentina, Uruguay, and Mexico. The architecture is tight and deliberate: reads go direct from the anon client to Supabase (RLS-protected), while all writes are funneled through Deno Edge Functions that enforce input validation, rate limiting via IP+UserAgent fingerprinting, and use the service role key server-side. A crowd-sourced moderation system auto-hides places with 3+ reports. The codebase is clean, well-tested (vitest), trilingual (ES/EN/PT), and has CI for function deployment.
analysis.project_type: Community-sourced donation point and volunteering directory — React SPA backed by Supabase BaaS

architecture.pattern: BaaS SPA — static frontend + Supabase as backend (DB + Auth + Edge Functions)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client with typed queries; no ORM. Reads hit Supabase anon API directly; writes go through Deno Edge Functions using the service role key.
architecture.api_design: Three Supabase Edge Functions (add-place, add-confirmation, report-place) handle all mutations. Reads are client-side queries to Supabase tables/views with RLS.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear: src/components for UI, src/hooks for data fetching/mutations, src/lib for pure logic (validation, rate limits, hours), src/i18n for translations, supabase/functions for server-side write handlers, db/migrations for schema.
architecture.modularity: High — shared validation and rate-limit logic is extracted into supabase/functions/_shared/ and reused across all three edge functions. Frontend hooks wrap all mutations behind a clean interface.
notable_patterns: Anonymous write pattern: all mutations go through edge functions to keep service role key server-side
Crowd-sourced moderation: active_places view hides places with >=3 claims automatically (SQL)
Rate limiting via SHA-256(IP|UserAgent) fingerprint stored in Postgres, enforced via RPC
Graceful degradation: on Supabase failure, UI falls back to hardcoded SEED_PLACES so the app stays usable
Trilingual i18n (ES/EN/PT) with custom hook, no external library

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ cleanly split into components/, hooks/, lib/, i18n/, types/, data/. supabase/functions/ has one directory per function plus _shared/. db/migrations/ holds hand-written SQL. No dead files or obvious cruft.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent. Files, functions, and types are self-explanatory (usePlaces, addPlaceViaFunction, enforceRateLimit, normalizeAddPlacePayload). The _shared/ naming convention mirrors Supabase conventions.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers local dev, env vars, local Supabase, and local edge-function serving with curl examples. Inline docs are sparse but code is readable enough. Docs/superpowers/plans contain lightweight design specs for recent features.
code_organization.reasoning: Well above hackathon average. The only gap keeping it from 5 is the absence of JSDoc on public functions and no architectural diagram, but the code structure is self-documenting.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service role key lives only in Supabase edge function environment (Deno.env). Frontend receives only the anon key via VITE_ env vars. README explicitly warns against exposing service role key via Vite.
production_readiness.security_notes: Rate limiting with dual keys (global + per-place) for all write operations. Input validation and type-narrowing in every edge function before DB access. RLS enabled on all tables. Place moderation auto-removes suspicious entries. Fingerprinting uses SHA-256(IP+UserAgent) — can be bypassed but is a reasonable mitigation for anonymous submissions.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Client fetches ALL places and ALL confirmations in two unbounded queries on every load — will degrade visibly past a few thousand rows. No pagination or server-side filtering. Supabase free tier has row limits. For a viral spike this is a real concern.
production_readiness.reasoning: The site is already live and serving real users. All write paths are secured server-side. The main production gap is no server-side logging/observability beyond Vercel Analytics, and the unbounded client-side data fetch won't scale past a few thousand places.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed, actively maintained project. 62 commits in 3 days from 4 contributors, real seed data for 17 physical locations, an actual live domain (apoyovenezuela.org), and evidence of iterative feature additions (hours, timezone, place_claims migration history).
maturity.reasoning: This is not scaffolding — it has real location data, a live URL, active schema migrations, vitest unit tests covering validation and mutation logic, and a CI pipeline for edge function deployment. Remarkable output for 3 days.

viability.reasoning: The project is already live and helping real people. Its narrow, well-executed scope (find where to donate/volunteer near you) is exactly what's needed in the first days after a disaster. The diaspora-first approach is smart given Venezuela's large international community. The main risk is scalability if it goes viral and the unbounded fetch pattern hits limits, but that is a tractable engineering problem.

key_features: Community-sourced directory of physical donation drop-off points and volunteering locations across the Venezuelan diaspora
Crowd-sourced moderation: places auto-hidden after 3 reports (SQL view, no admin needed)
Community confirmation system: users log recent visits/donations to validate a place is still active
Open-hours tracking with timezone-aware 'open now' filter
Graceful degradation to hardcoded seed data when Supabase is unavailable
Rate-limited anonymous writes via Supabase Edge Functions (no user accounts required)
analysis.merge_potential: Low overlap with other projects. This is a diaspora-facing donation logistics tool, not an in-Venezuela rescue/shelter/medical tool. Could complement shelter-mapping or aid-logistics projects as a supply-side counterpart, but is independently deployable and already live.
red_flags: Client fetches ALL places + ALL confirmations in two unbounded queries — will degrade at scale with no pagination or server-side filtering
Rate limiting fingerprint (SHA-256 of IP+UserAgent) is bypassable with VPN rotation or spoofed user agents — moderation is automated but could be gamed
No admin UI to review reported places before they're auto-hidden — legitimate places could be mass-reported and vanish
GitHub Actions CI only deploys edge functions, not the frontend — frontend deploy pipeline is not visible in repo (presumably manual or connected via Vercel dashboard)
No structured logging or error monitoring (e.g., Sentry) — silent failures in edge functions beyond what Supabase logs capture
